stem: In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of the data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean?
solutionStandard: By the empirical rule, about 68% of the data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean.

stem: Scores on a test are normally distributed with mean 500 and standard deviation 100. What percent of scores are above 600?
solutionStandard: 600 is 1 SD above the mean. 50% of scores are above the mean; of the 68% within 1 SD, half (34%) are between the mean and 600. Above 600: \(50-34=16\%\).

stem: In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of the data falls between the mean and 2 standard deviations above the mean?
solutionStandard: 95% falls within 2 SD of the mean (symmetric), so half of that, 47.5%, lies between the mean and 2 SD above it.

stem: A data set is normally distributed.
quantityA: The percent of data within 2 standard deviations of the mean
quantityB: The percent of data within 1 standard deviation of the mean
solutionStandard: Within 2 SD is about 95%, and within 1 SD is about 68%. Since \(95>68\), Quantity A is greater.

stem: A distribution has mean 100 and standard deviation 10. What value is exactly 2 standard deviations below the mean?
In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of the data lies below 1 standard deviation below the mean?
solutionStandard: By symmetry, the percent below 1 SD below the mean equals the percent above 1 SD above the mean: \(50-34=16\%\).

stem: Which of the following are true for a normal distribution? Select all that apply.

options.text: The mean, median, and mode are equal
The distribution is symmetric about the mean
Exactly half the data is above the mean
All data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean

solutionStandard: (A), (B), and (C) are all defining properties of a normal distribution. (D) is false — only about 68% falls within 1 SD; the rest lies farther out.

stem: A normal distribution has mean 50 and standard deviation 5. What percent of the data falls between 45 and 55?
solutionStandard: 45 and 55 are exactly 1 SD below and above the mean, so about 68% of the data falls between them.

stem: In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of the data falls between 1 and 2 standard deviations above the mean?
solutionStandard: Between the mean and 2 SD above is 47.5%; between the mean and 1 SD above is 34%. The band from 1 to 2 SD above: \(47.5-34=13.5\%\).

stem: Distribution X has mean 200 and standard deviation 20. Distribution Y has mean 200 and standard deviation 30. Both are normal.
quantityA: The percent of Distribution X's data between 180 and 220
quantityB: The percent of Distribution Y's data between 180 and 220
solutionStandard: For X, 180 to 220 spans exactly 1 SD each way (SD=20), capturing about 68%. For Y (SD=30), 180 to 220 spans less than 1 SD each way, capturing less than 68%. Quantity A is greater.

stem: Scores are normally distributed with mean {{m}} and standard deviation {{s}}. What value is exactly 1 standard deviation above the mean?
In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of the data falls within 3 standard deviations of the mean?
solutionStandard: By the empirical rule, about 99.7% of the data falls within 3 standard deviations of the mean.

stem: Scores are normally distributed with mean {{m}} and standard deviation {{s}}. What percent of scores are below {{m-s}}?
solutionStandard: {{m-s}} is 1 SD below the mean. About 34% lies between it and the mean, and 50% lies below the mean overall, so below {{m-s}}: \(50-34=16\%\).

stem: In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of the data lies more than 2 standard deviations from the mean (above OR below)?
solutionStandard: About 95% of the data lies within 2 SD of the mean, so the remaining \(100-95=5\%\) lies farther than 2 SD away (split between both tails).

stem: A normal distribution has mean {{m}} and standard deviation {{s}}.
solutionStandard: Both {{m+s}} and {{m-s}} are exactly 1 SD from the mean, and a normal distribution is symmetric about its mean, so the tail beyond 1 SD above equals the tail beyond 1 SD below (each about 16%). The quantities are always equal.

stem: A normal distribution has mean {{m}} and standard deviation {{s}}. What percent of the data falls between {{m-2*s}} and {{m+2*s}}?
solutionStandard: {{m-2*s}} and {{m+2*s}} are exactly 2 SD below and above the mean, so about 95% of the data falls between them.

stem: A value lies exactly 3 standard deviations above the mean of a normal distribution. Which of the following are true? Select all that apply.

options.text: It is an unusually extreme (rare) value
More than 99% of the data lies below it
Exactly 3% of the data lies above it
No data point can ever exceed it

solutionStandard: About 99.7% of data lies within 3 SD of the mean, so roughly 99.85% lies below this value — confirming (A) and (B). (C) is false (the true figure is about 0.15%, not 3%). (D) is false — a normal distribution's tails extend without bound, so values beyond 3 SD are rare but not impossible.

stem: A normal distribution has mean {{m}} and standard deviation {{s}}. What percent of the data falls between {{m+s}} and {{m+2*s}}?
solutionStandard: Between the mean and 2 SD above is about 47.5%; between the mean and 1 SD above is about 34%. The band from 1 to 2 SD above: \(47.5-34=13.5\%\). This percentage doesn't depend on the actual mean or standard deviation — only on how many SDs away the boundaries are.

stem: Distribution X has mean 500 and standard deviation 50. Distribution Y has mean 500 and standard deviation 80. Both are normal.
quantityA: The percent of Distribution X's data above 600
quantityB: The percent of Distribution Y's data above 600
solutionStandard: For X, 600 is \((600-500)/50=2\) SD above the mean, a relatively far tail (~2.5% beyond it). For Y, 600 is only \((600-500)/80=1.25\) SD above the mean, much closer to the bulk of the data, so more than 2.5% lies beyond it. Since a smaller z-score always corresponds to a larger tail, Quantity B is greater.

stem: A normal distribution has mean \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\), where \(\sigma>0\).
quantityA: The percent of the data within \(\sigma\) of \(\mu\)
solutionStandard: The empirical rule's percentages (68%, 95%, 99.7%) are properties of the normal shape itself, expressed in units of standard deviation — they hold for every normal distribution regardless of the specific values of \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\). The quantities are always equal.
